system_prompt:
You are Hermes Agent, an intelligent AI assistant created by Nous Research. You are helpful, knowledgeable, and direct. You assist users with a wide range of tasks including answering questions, writing and editing code, analyzing information, creative work, and executing actions via your tools. You communicate clearly, admit uncertainty when appropriate, and prioritize being genuinely useful over being verbose unless otherwise directed below. Be targeted and efficient in your exploration and investigations.

Conversation started: Sunday, April 12, 2026 12:48 AM
Model: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
Provider: openrouter

You are on a text messaging communication platform, Telegram. Please do not use markdown as it does not render. You can send media files natively: to deliver a file to the user, include MEDIA:/absolute/path/to/file in your response. Images (.png, .jpg, .webp) appear as photos, audio (.ogg) sends as voice bubbles, and videos (.mp4) play inline. You can also include image URLs in markdown format ![alt](url) and they will be sent as native photos.

function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/AGENTS.md]
# Law Firm Wiki — Compiler Schema

> This file is the specification for the LLM that compiles activity logs
> into structured knowledge articles. Read it in full before any compile
> or query operation.

## What You Are

You are a knowledge compiler for a personal injury law firm. Your job is
to read raw case activity logs and extract reusable institutional knowledge
— the process, pitfalls, patterns, and best practices that make a PI
practice work.

You are NOT creating a case database. You are building a **wiki** — a
structured collection of concept articles about HOW PI cases work, derived
from evidence in real cases.

## The Three Operations

### 1. COMPILE (ingest raw → update wiki)

Read activity logs and case files. For each meaningful pattern you identify:

1. Check `index.md` — does a concept article already exist?
2. If yes → UPDATE it: add new evidence, adjust confidence, update Related
3. If no → CREATE it: full article per the Article Schema below
4. If two concepts interact → create/update a Connection article
5. Update `index.md` with any new or modified articles
6. Append a summary to `log.md`

**What to extract:**
- Process patterns (what happens and in what order)
- Decision points (why certain choices were made)
- Problems and resolutions (what went wrong, how it was fixed)
- Timeline patterns (how long things actually take)
- Communication patterns (what works with adjusters, clients, providers)
- Edge cases (unusual situations and how they were handled)
- Anti-patterns (things that caused problems)

**What to IGNORE:**
- Individual client PII (names, SSN, DOB, addresses, phone numbers)
- Specific dollar amounts tied to identifiable cases
- Attorney-client privileged communications
- Raw email addresses and phone numbers
- Replace specifics with anonymized references: "Case A", "Case B", or
  descriptive patterns: "in cases involving [treatment gap > 30 days]"

**Confidence scoring:**
- high: Pattern observed in 5+ cases with consistent outcomes
- medium: Pattern observed in 2-4 cases
- low: Single case observation, or conflicting evidence

### 2. QUERY (ask questions → synthesize answers)

1. Read `index.md` to identify 3-10 relevant concept articles
2. Read those articles in full
3. Synthesize an answer with [[wikilink]] citations
4. If the answer reveals a gap → note it in the answer
5. Optionally file the Q&A back into `wiki/qa/`

### 3. LINT (health check)

Run these checks and write results to `wiki/reports/`:
- **Broken wikilinks**: Links that point to non-existent articles
- **Orphan articles**: Concepts with zero inbound links
- **Phase coverage**: Which PHASE_DAG phases have sparse knowledge
- **Stale articles**: Source data changed since last compile
- **Contradictions**: Articles that contain conflicting guidance

## Article Schema

### Concept Article (wiki/concepts/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Human-Readable Title
slug: kebab-case-slug
phase: [phase_0_onboarding]    # which PHASE_DAG phases this applies to
category: one-of-below
tags: [tag1, tag2, tag3]
confidence: high               # high/medium/low
evidence_count: 5              # number of cases with supporting evidence
created: YYYY-MM-DD
updated: YYYY-MM-DD
related:                       # other concept slugs
  - related-concept-one
  - related-concept-two
---

# Title

> One-paragraph summary (2-3 sentences max)

## Pattern

Detailed explanation of the pattern, process, or concept.

## Evidence

Anonymized observations from source cases:
- "In N cases where [condition], [outcome]"
- "When [action was taken], [result]"

## Agent Guidance

Actionable instructions for paralegal agents:
1. Step one
2. Step two
3. Step three

## Pitfalls

- Common mistake one
- Common mistake two

## Related

- [[related-concept-one]] — how it connects
- [[related-concept-two]] — how it connects
```

### Categories

- `process`: Standard workflows and procedures
- `adjuster-tactics`: Insurance company behavior patterns
- `edge-cases`: Unusual situations requiring special handling
- `phase-knowledge`: Phase-specific best practices
- `communication`: Client/adjuster/provider communication patterns
- `financial`: Billing, liens, settlements, funding
- `medical`: Treatment patterns, records, chronologies
- `legal`: Litigation, discovery, depositions, trial
- `tools`: Software, systems, and process tools

### Connection Article (wiki/connections/*.md)

Same schema but `category: connection` and the body explains how
two or more concepts interact. Example:

```
treatment-gaps-affect-demand-value.md
→ Links: treatment-gap-defense + demand-timing-strategy
→ Insight: Cases with documented gap explanations recovered 80% of
  expected value vs 50% for unexplained gaps
```

### Q&A Article (wiki/qa/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Question text
slug: question-slug
date: YYYY-MM-DD
sources:                       # concept articles consulted
  - concept-slug-one
  - concept-slug-two
---

# Question

[Full question text]

## Answer

[Synthesized answer with [[wikilink]] citations]

## Gaps Identified

[Any knowledge gaps the question revealed]
```

## Index Schema (wiki/index.md)

The master catalog. This is the LLM's entry point for all queries.

```markdown
# Law Firm Wiki — Index

Last compiled: YYYY-MM-DD | Articles: N | Connections: N

## By Phase

### Phase 0: Onboarding
| Article | Category | Confidence | Evidence |
|---------|----------|------------|----------|
| [[concepts/onboarding-pitfalls]] | phase-knowledge | high | 15 |

### Phase 1: File Setup
...

## By Category

### Adjuster Tactics
| Article | Phase | Confidence |
|---------|-------|------------|
| [[concepts/treatment-gap-defense]] | 2,3 | high |
...

## Recent Updates

| Date | Article | Action |
|------|---------|--------|
| 2026-04-12 | treatment-gap-defense | created |
```

## Activity Log Format Reference

Activity logs live at `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/*.md` and have this structure:

```yaml
---
schema_version: 2
case_id: uuid
date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
time: "HH:MM:SS"         # optional
category: legal|correspondence|imported|financial|medical|task
subcategory: email|filevine_negotiation|outbound_call|...
---

# Category — Date

**Case:** [[cases/slug/slug|Client Name]]
**Source:** description

[Free-form content: emails, notes, call summaries, task descriptions]
```

### Subcategory Distribution (21K+ logs)
- email: 3,078
- filevine_negotiation: 1,620
- outbound_call: 890
- missed_call: 780
- filevine_treatment: 717
- filevine_demandinprogress: 645
- demand: 534
- filevine_filesetup: 369
- phase_change: 254
- filevine_uim: 179
- inbound_call: 137
- esignature: 78
- call_summary: 10

## PHASE_DAG Reference

The case lifecycle has 8 phases:
- Phase 0: Onboarding (contract, HIPAA, intake)
- Phase 1: File Setup (insurance claims, provider setup)
- Phase 2: Treatment (monitoring, records collection)
- Phase 3: Demand (drafting, review, sending)
- Phase 4: Negotiation (offers, counters, strategy)
- Phase 5: Settlement (agreement, disbursement)
- Phase 6: Lien Resolution (PIP waterfall, lien negotiation)
- Phase 7: Litigation (complaint, discovery, trial)
- Phase 8: Closed

## Rules

1. NEVER include identifiable client information in wiki articles
2. Anonymize all case references (use "Case A", "Case B", or descriptive patterns)
3. Every article must have at least one [[wikilink]] to a related concept
4. Update index.md whenever you create or modify an article
5. Append to log.md whenever you compile
6. Confidence scores must reflect actual evidence count
7. When in doubt about a pattern's validity, use confidence: low
8. Prefer specific actionable guidance over vague advice
9. Group knowledge by PHASE_DAG phase when possible
10. Flag contradictions explicitly — don't silently resolve them

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/CLAUDE.md]
# FirmVault — Agent Orientation

> **Read this in full at the start of every session.** It names the thesis, the non-negotiable rules, and where to find the authoritative docs. Short deliberately — use the imports below for detail.

## What this repo is

FirmVault is the **development environment for an AI paralegal backend** intended to eventually be sold to other personal-injury law firms. This specific repo is Whaley Law Firm's own instance; the architecture we prove here becomes the product.

Two parallel product tracks exist:

1. **This repo** — the GitHub-native paralegal backend: markdown vault, `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, `SKILL.md` library, runtime (materializer + worker), intake pipeline.
2. **Separate Huly fork** (not in this repo) — the lawyer-facing UI. This repo's "UI" is for operators, not lawyers.

## The thesis

**"The firm as a code repo."** Law firm work is repeatable tasks + templates + rules, which is exactly what code is. Put the firm's work into primitives agents already excel at (issues, branches, PRs, diffs) and you get a much better AI experience than any traditional PMS bolt-on.

## Core architectural principles

1. **The vault is a shadow, not the source of truth.** Real files (signed PDFs, audio, emails, faxes) live on the firm's own storage (their server, Dropbox, Box, Drive). The markdown in `cases/<slug>/` is a PHI-masked projection of those files.
2. **Intake is a pipeline, not an agent**: document arrives → real file placed on user's storage → converted to markdown → PHI stripped → opens a PR against this repo. The PR is the first human-in-the-loop gate.
3. **Git is the deliberate interface.** Every agent action is a commit or PR. Free audit trail, free approval gates, trivial rollback. Agents *never* touch real files — only their masked shadows.
4. **Markdown, not a graph DB.** We tried FalkorDB and abandoned it. LLM agents are empirically more reliable on plain text + grep than on Cypher queries. This is a deliberate choice, not laziness.
5. **PHI never lives in the vault.** Placeholders like `{{client_ssn}}` and `{{dob}}` reference secrets held in the user's own infrastructure. At render time, deterministic code (not the model) substitutes real values. The model never sees raw PII.
6. **The vault is the only state store for this layer.** No external database. No per-case JSON files. No FalkorDB. No `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`. See `@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md` for the authoritative mapping.

## Do NOT do these things

These are actively deprecated. If you see them in old code or docs, treat them as bugs.

- **Do not reference FalkorDB, Cypher, `graph_client`, `graph_manager`, or `run_cypher_query`.** All removed. Everything reads the vault.
- **Do not reference per-case JSON files** (`overview.json`, `contacts.json`, `insurance.json`, `liens.json`, `expenses.json`, `medical_providers.json`, `case_state.json`). Deleted.
- **Do not reference `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`** or any absolute-path scaffolding. Everything is repo-relative.
- **Do not edit content between `<!-- roscoe-medical-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-medical-end -->` or `<!-- roscoe-insurance-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-insurance-end -->` markers.** Those blocks are owned by the import scripts and will be overwritten on next sync.
- **Do not invent new vault paths.** If the thing you want to write doesn't have a home in `DATA_CONTRACT.md`, update the contract first (same commit), then write. Drift between contract and reality is the bug.
- **Do not create top-level dashboards, reports, or state files outside the vault layout**. Operator views read the vault via GitHub API or git; they don't create parallel state.

## Where the authoritative docs live

Import these at launch — they are the contract, not me paraphrasing:

@DESIGN.md

@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md

@MEMORY.md

Also non-imported but worth knowing:

- `skills.tools.workflows/workflows/PHASE_DAG.yaml` — machine-readable phase definitions, landmarks, and predicate vocabulary. The materializer reads this.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/README.md` — how the materializer and worker agents drive the task queue.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/task_schema.md` — the contract for task issues (labels, frontmatter, lifecycle).
- `Templates/INDEX.md` — the firm template library (73 slugified templates grouped by category).
- `skills.tools.workflows/Skills/*/SKILL.md` — 42 modernized skills in Anthropic Agent Skills format. Exemplars: `lien-management`, `deposition-prep`, `deposition-strategy`, `pip-application`.

## Slug rules (critical for wikilinks)

From `DATA_CONTRACT.md` §4:

- Lowercase
- Apostrophes and curly quotes **stripped** (no replacement char): `K'daya` → `kdaya`
- `&` → `and`
- All other non-alphanumeric runs collapsed to a single hyphen
- Leading/trailing hyphens stripped

Mismatched slugs break wikilinks across the vault. Slug before you write.

## Runtime model (what's being built)

The system is being turned from passive documentation into an active work-driving loop:

```
Vault (cases/*) ← materializer (cron) → GitHub Issues ← worker (event) → Vault
```

- **Materializer** walks open cases, reads each case's `landmarks:` frontmatter + `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, opens a GitHub Issue for every unsatisfied landmark whose task template preconditions are met. Already prototyped as a prompt in `runtime/materializer_prompt.md`.
- **Worker** is triggered by the `status:ready` label on an issue, claims it, loads the named skill, runs it, commits vault changes that reference the task_id, and flips the issue status. Prototyped in `runtime/worker_prompt.md`.
- **Source of truth** for whether a landmark is satisfied is `case.frontmatter.landmarks.<id>` — the materializer checks this first and only falls back to evaluating a predicate if the key is missing.

## Working branch

All in-progress work lives on `claude/import-cases-from-drive-xiQgL`. Do not push directly to `main` without an explicit request.

## When in doubt

- Read `DATA_CONTRACT.md` before writing to the vault.
- Read `PHASE_DAG.yaml` before touching workflow/landmark logic.
- Read `MEMORY.md` to find out what's in flight and what's pending.
- Check the last 3–5 commits on the working branch to see the most recent decisions.

# Premises Liability Case Patterns

> Premises liability (PL) cases — typically slip-and-fall incidents at commercial properties — follow a different trajectory than auto accident cases. They are more likely to proceed to litigation, involve property owner defense counsel early, and require more extensive expert work (FCE, IME, vocational/economic experts). The liability analysis focuses on property owner notice and maintenance rather than traffic fault.

## Pattern

PL cases differ from auto accident cases in several key ways:

### Liability Analysis
- **Property owner notice**: Was the hazardous condition known to the owner? How long did it exist?
- **Maintenance records**: Were inspection and maintenance protocols followed?
- **Incident reports**: Was an incident report filed at the time of the fall?
- **Surveillance footage**: Commercial properties may have video of the incident; preservation requests should be sent immediately

# File Transfer from Prior Counsel

> When a client transfers from another firm, obtaining the prior counsel's file is a critical prerequisite before any case work can proceed. This process frequently encounters delays due to unresponsive prior counsel, requiring persistent multi-channel follow-up over days or weeks.

## Pattern

When a client comes to the firm after previously being represented elsewhere, the prior firm's complete file must be obtained before the case can move forward. The typical workflow is:

1. Identify the prior firm and case manager contact info
2. Send an initial email requesting the file
3. Follow up by phone if no response within 1-2 business days
4. Escalate (daily calls, multiple contacts at the prior firm)
5. Enlist client/family assistance if prior firm remains unresponsive
6. Eventually receive file and perform cursory review

# Litigation Process Patterns

> When PI cases proceed to litigation, they enter a structured legal process involving complaint filing, discovery (interrogatories, document production), depositions, motions practice, and potential mediation or trial. Litigation dramatically increases case complexity, timeline, and administrative burden on the paralegal team.

## Pattern

The litigation lifecycle follows a predictable sequence:

### 1. Complaint Filing
- Draft complaint with interrogatories (IROG)
- File with the circuit court
- Check service of process on defendant

# Client Frustration Escalation Pattern

> PI clients exhibit a predictable pattern of escalating frustration when they feel the firm is not communicating with them. This pattern typically begins with polite check-ins, escalates to demands for immediate response, and can culminate in threats to change attorneys. Preventing this escalation through proactive communication is more effective than responding to it after it begins.

## Pattern

The escalation follows a predictable trajectory:

### Stage 1: Polite Inquiry
- Client texts or calls asking for a general update
- "I would like to know where we are on this case"
- "Just checking in"

### Stage 2: Urgent Demand

# Unreachable Client Patterns

> Some PI clients are persistently difficult to reach — their voicemail is full, they don't return calls, and emails go unanswered. This pattern is distinct from client frustration (where the client is actively trying to reach the firm). Unreachable clients create a bottleneck because the firm needs their input to proceed with intake, treatment confirmation, and case management. Persistent multi-channel contact attempts are required, sometimes over weeks.

## Pattern

### Common Unreachability Signals
1. **Voicemail full**: The client's voicemail box is full, preventing the firm from leaving a message
2. **No answer, no callback**: Multiple outbound calls with no answer and no return call
3. **Phone number changes**: Client changes phone numbers without notifying the firm
4. **Email unresponsive**: Welcome emails and information requests go unanswered

### Multi-Channel Escalation

# Medical Records and Bills Collection

> Collecting complete medical records and bills from all treatment providers is a prerequisite for demand preparation. This process involves sending HIPAA authorizations, formal record/bill requests via fax and email, and persistent follow-up when providers are slow to respond. Gaps in records collection directly delay case resolution.

## Pattern

The records collection workflow follows this sequence:

1. **Provider Identification**: Confirm all treatment providers with the client, including ER, PCP, specialists, imaging
2. **Request Preparation**: Generate HIPAA medical authorization forms and formal records/billing request letters for each provider
3. **Request Delivery**: Send via fax (using RingCentral or similar) and/or email to the provider's records department
4. **Response Tracking**: Track which providers have responded with records vs. bills vs. both
5. **Follow-Up**: If no response within 2-3 weeks, follow up

# Case Phase Transitions

> PI cases follow a defined lifecycle: Lead -> Potential New Client -> File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand in Progress -> Negotiation -> Settlement. Each transition has specific prerequisites and is triggered by specific events. Understanding these transitions is critical for case management automation.

## Pattern

Observed phase transitions across cases:

### Lead -> File Setup (or via Potential New Client)
- **Trigger**: Intake docs signed, client info received
- **Prerequisites**: Signed fee agreement, HIPAA authorization, basic client info
- **Actions on transition**: Create welcome call task, send welcome email, set up insurance claims, begin records requests
- **Timeline**: Same day to a few days after initial contact

# Intake and Onboarding Workflow

> The intake process converts a potential client into an active case. It involves collecting personal information, executing the retainer agreement, setting up insurance claims, identifying medical providers, and establishing communication channels. The workflow must be completed rapidly to preserve evidence and begin the claims process.

## Pattern

Standard intake workflow observed across cases:

1. **Initial Contact**: Client (or family member) reaches out, usually by phone. Case manager gathers preliminary accident details, injuries, and insurance information.
2. **Information Request**: Firm requests: Name, DOB, SSN, phone number, copy of health insurance card, names/addresses of hospital and PCP.
3. **Contract Execution**: Intake documents sent via VineSign (electronic signature platform) for completion. Documents include fee agreement, HIPAA authorization, and other intake forms.
4. **Welcome Call**: Once docs are signed, firm conducts a welcome call to confirm treatment providers and introduce the client to the process.
5. **Phase Change**: Case moves from Lead/PNC to File Setup.
6. **Insurance Setup**: Send Letters of Representation to both BI (at-fault) and PIP (client's own) carriers. Request acknowledgment.

# Workers' Compensation Case Patterns

> Workers' compensation cases differ fundamentally from personal injury cases in their procedural framework, involved parties, and resolution mechanisms. WC cases are governed by state Department of Workers' Compensation (DWC) rules rather than civil court procedures, involve employer's WC insurance carriers rather than at-fault BI carriers, and resolve through administrative proceedings (Benefit Review Conferences) rather than jury trials.

## Pattern

### Key Differences from PI Cases
1. **Intake**: CaseOpp system may use the wrong contract type (PI vs WC) — must verify and re-send WC-specific intake documents
2. **Insurance Setup**: LOR sent to WC adjuster (not BI/PIP). DWC insurance lookup required to identify the WC carrier
3. **Treatment Monitoring**: Extended treatment periods common (2+ years). Client may undergo surgery, PT, injections, and still be in active treatment. Treatment includes hardware placement (plates, screws)
4. **Administrative Proceedings**: BRC (Benefit Review Conference) hearings are telephonic, scheduled by DWC, with assigned passcodes. BRCs are frequently rescheduled
5. **Forms**: DWC Form 106 required for various proceedings — sent to client via DocuSign for execution per defense counsel request
6. **Defense Counsel**: Employer/carrier represented by defense firm that exchanges correspondence about Form 106, IME scheduling, and case status

# Workers' Compensation Claim Patterns

> Workers' compensation (WC) cases follow a fundamentally different process than auto accident PI cases. WC claims involve an employer's insurance carrier that must approve treatment, authorize specific providers, and pay Temporary Total Disability (TTD) benefits. Unlike BI claims where the firm collects records and sends a demand, WC cases require ongoing adjuster interaction to authorize each step of treatment.

## Pattern

### WC Intake Differences
1. **Employer notification**: Unlike auto accidents, the client must have notified their employer of the workplace injury
2. **WC carrier identification**: The employer's WC carrier (not the client's personal auto/health insurer) is the primary claim target
3. **LOR sent to WC carrier**: Letter of Representation is sent to the WC adjuster via email and fax
4. **Claim acceptance**: The WC adjuster must formally accept the claim before treatment is authorized

# Client Communication Through Family Members

> In many PI cases, the primary point of contact is a family member (spouse, parent) rather than the injured client directly. This pattern creates both efficiencies (an engaged advocate) and risks (communication gaps, authorization requirements). Managing these relationships requires clear protocols around authorization, documentation, and expectation-setting.

## Pattern

Two common scenarios emerge:

**Spouse as Primary Contact**: The spouse handles all communication, forwards medical documents, asks status questions, and relays information to the injured client. The injured client may have signed the retainer but is less directly involved in day-to-day case management.

**Parent with POA**: When a client is incarcerated or otherwise unavailable, a parent with Power of Attorney may serve as the authorized representative. The POA document must be obtained and verified before the firm can discuss case details.

In both scenarios, the family member tends to:
- Be more proactive about asking for updates
- Forward bills and medical documents as they arrive

# Statute of Limitations Monitoring

> The statute of limitations (SOL) is the absolute deadline for filing a lawsuit. In Kentucky PI cases, this is typically 2 years from the date of accident. Approaching the SOL without resolution requires escalation to the attorney for a decision: settle at current offer, file suit, or withdraw. SOL monitoring is automated in Filevine with countdown alerts.

## Pattern

SOL monitoring follows a countdown pattern with escalation triggers:

- **180 days out**: Initial alert — ensure case is actively being worked
- **90 days out**: Elevated alert — settlement or suit filing decision must be imminent
- **30 days out**: Critical alert — attorney must decide: file suit, accept offer, or take other action

Cases that are still in negotiation as the SOL approaches create high-risk situations. The firm must either:
1. Reach settlement before the SOL expires
2. File a lawsuit to preserve the claim (can still settle after filing)

# Case Decline and Closing Workflow

> Not all retained cases proceed to demand and settlement. Some cases must be declined after intake reveals insufficient coverage — most commonly when the at-fault driver has no BI insurance and the client has no UM/UIM coverage, leaving only PIP. Declining a case requires attorney authorization, a closing letter to the client, and notification to all parties (BI carrier, PIP carrier, lien holders).

## Pattern

### Evaluation Triggers for Decline
1. **No BI coverage**: At-fault driver is uninsured or has no bodily injury liability coverage
2. **No UM/UIM coverage**: Client's own policy does not include uninsured/underinsured motorist coverage
3. **PIP-only recovery**: Only available coverage is PIP (medical payments), which does not include pain and suffering
4. **UM rejection on file**: Client's insurer can produce a signed UM rejection form
5. **Case manager flags the issue**: "No BI or UM only PIP through KAC" — paralegal identifies the coverage gap and flags for attorney

# Expert Reports and Evaluations

> Expert reports — including Functional Capacity Evaluations (FCE), Independent Medical Examinations (IME), and vocational/economic analyses — are critical for establishing damages in PI cases, especially in litigation. They provide objective documentation of physical limitations, treatment outcomes, and economic impact of injuries. These evaluations require significant scheduling coordination and expense management.

## Pattern

### Functional Capacity Evaluation (FCE)
- Ordered when objective measurement of physical limitations is needed
- Performed by specialized providers (e.g., Biokinetics through Commonwealth IME)
- Client must travel to the evaluation site (may be in a different city)
- Cost: ~$550+ per evaluation
- Client may reschedule on their own, which delays the case
- Report typically received within 2-3 weeks of evaluation

# Dental Injury Case Patterns

> Auto accidents that cause dental and jaw injuries (TMJ, tooth fractures, jaw pain) follow a distinct treatment pattern compared to typical soft-tissue PI cases. Dental treatment involves multiple specialist providers (general dentist, endodontist, oral surgeon), staged procedures spanning 12+ months (root canals, extractions, implants, crowns), and creates unique challenges for case timeline management — particularly the risk of treatment extending near or beyond the SOL.

## Pattern

### Provider Chain
Dental injury cases involve a chain of specialized providers:
1. **ER/PCP**: Initial complaint of jaw pain and facial injury documented
2. **Imaging**: X-rays/orthopantogram to assess jaw and dental damage
3. **General Dentist**: Initial assessment, TMJ treatment, temporary solutions
4. **Endodontist**: Root canal treatment for damaged teeth
5. **Oral & Maxillofacial Surgeon**: Extraction, implant placement, bone grafting

    17|
    18|# Premises Liability Case Patterns
    19|
    20|> Premises liability (PL) cases — typically slip-and-fall incidents at commercial properties — follow a different trajectory than auto accident cases. They are more likely to proceed to litigation, involve property owner defense counsel early, and require more extensive expert work (FCE, IME, vocational/economic experts). The liability analysis focuses on property owner notice and maintenance rather than traffic fault.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|PL cases differ from auto accident cases in several key ways:
    25|
    26|### Liability Analysis
    27|- **Property owner notice**: Was the hazardous condition known to the owner? How long did it exist?
    28|- **Maintenance records**: Were inspection and maintenance protocols followed?
    29|- **Incident reports**: Was an incident report filed at the time of the fall?
    30|- **Surveillance footage**: Commercial properties may have video of the incident; preservation requests should be sent immediately
    31|- **Third-party defendants**: Banks, stores, and other commercial entities typically have dedicated defense counsel (not insurance adjusters)
    32|
    33|### Litigation Tendency
    34|- PL cases are more likely to proceed to litigation than auto accident cases
    35|- Defense counsel for commercial property owners may aggressively litigate through discovery, depositions, and motions practice
    36|- Mediation is commonly attempted before trial
    37|
    38|### Expert Work
    39|- **Functional Capacity Evaluations (FCE)**: Used to document physical limitations from the injury
    40|- **Independent Medical Examinations (IME)**: May be requested by either side; amendments may be needed as treatment progresses
    41|- **Economist/Vocational Reports**: When the injury affects earning capacity, vocational economics experts are retained
    42|
    43|### Timeline
    44|- PL cases tend to run significantly longer than auto accident cases (3-6+ years observed vs. 1-2 years for typical MVA)
    45|- Discovery deadlines, motions practice, and mediation scheduling extend the timeline
    46|
    47|## Evidence
    48|
    49|- In a premises liability case (slip-and-fall at a commercial bank, 2019 incident), the case progressed through litigation with complaint filing, interrogatories (IROG), discovery, depositions, mediation scheduling, IME, FCE, and economist report requests — spanning 2019-2025+. Defense counsel for the property owner actively participated in discovery and motions practice. Mediation was scheduled then cancelled. The case required certified medical records for litigation (not just demand copies).
    50|- The same case involved multiple medical providers across orthopedics, neurosurgery, PCP, and imaging facilities, with the attorney directing additional imaging (MRI) based on IME findings.
    51|- Tax documentation was required for economic damages claims, and the defense counsel specifically challenged tax document accuracy (wrong year provided).
    52|
    53|## Agent Guidance
    54|
    55|1. At intake for PL cases, immediately send a preservation letter to the property owner requesting incident reports, maintenance logs, and surveillance footage
    56|2. Identify the property owner's insurance carrier AND their defense counsel — PL cases often have dedicated law firms representing the property owner
    57|3. Plan for litigation from the start: request certified records (not just copies) from medical providers
    58|4. Budget for expert expenses early: FCE ($500-600+), IME, vocational/economic reports
    59|5. Track discovery deadlines meticulously — interrogatories, document production, deposition scheduling
    60|6. Coordinate mediation scheduling between all parties (mediator, defense counsel, client) well in advance
    61|7. When the attorney directs additional medical evaluation based on IME/FCE findings, prioritize scheduling
    62|
    63|## Pitfalls
    64|
    65|- Treating a PL case like an auto accident case (PL is more complex and litigation-heavy)
    66|- Failing to request surveillance footage promptly — it may be overwritten
    67|- Not requesting certified records for litigation (regular copies may be insufficient for court)
    68|- Providing incorrect documents to defense counsel (e.g., wrong tax year) — verify all documents before production
    69|- Not coordinating expert evaluations (FCE, IME) early enough — scheduling can take weeks/months
    70|- Allowing mediation to be cancelled without promptly rescheduling
    71|
    72|## Related
    73|
    74|- [[litigation-process-patterns]] — PL cases frequently enter litigation
    75|- [[medical-records-collection]] — PL cases require certified records for litigation
    76|- [[expert-reports-and-evaluations]] — FCE, IME, and vocational reports are common in PL
    77|- [[case-phase-transitions]] — PL cases may skip demand phase and go directly to litigation
    78|", "total_lines": 77, "file_size": 5063, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    14|---
    15|
    16|# File Transfer from Prior Counsel
    17|
    18|> When a client transfers from another firm, obtaining the prior counsel's file is a critical prerequisite before any case work can proceed. This process frequently encounters delays due to unresponsive prior counsel, requiring persistent multi-channel follow-up over days or weeks.
    19|
    20|## Pattern
    21|
    22|When a client comes to the firm after previously being represented elsewhere, the prior firm's complete file must be obtained before the case can move forward. The typical workflow is:
    23|
    24|1. Identify the prior firm and case manager contact info
    25|2. Send an initial email requesting the file
    26|3. Follow up by phone if no response within 1-2 business days
    27|4. Escalate (daily calls, multiple contacts at the prior firm)
    28|5. Enlist client/family assistance if prior firm remains unresponsive
    29|6. Eventually receive file and perform cursory review
    30|
    31|The entire process can take 1-2 weeks even with persistent follow-up.
    32|
    33|## Evidence
    34|
    35|- In Case A, the firm called and emailed the prior counsel (a large national firm) daily for over a week with no response. Multiple contacts at the prior firm were tried. The client's family member was eventually asked to assist. The file was received approximately 11 days after the first request.
    36|- Prior counsel may have already sent a demand, taken certain negotiation steps, or received offers that the new firm needs to understand before proceeding.
    37|
    38|## Agent Guidance
    39|
    40|1. On day 1 of a transfer case, identify the prior firm's case manager by name and direct contact info
    41|2. Send a formal written request (email) for the complete client file on day 1
    42|3. If no response within 48 hours, begin daily phone calls
    43|4. Log every contact attempt in the activity log
    44|5. After 5 business days of no response, ask the client or their authorized representative to contact the prior firm directly
    45|6. Once the file is received, do a cursory review and report key findings to the attorney: existing demand, offers, treatment gaps, outstanding liens, pre-settlement funding
    46|7. Update the case file with all prior-counsel information before moving to negotiation
    47|
    48|## Pitfalls
    49|
    50|- Assuming the prior firm will respond quickly — large firms often have slow internal processes
    51|- Not documenting contact attempts — this creates gaps in the activity log
    52|- Proceeding with negotiation before understanding what the prior firm already did (e.g., a demand may have already been sent)
    53|- Missing pre-settlement funding obligations that the prior firm arranged
    54|
    55|## Related
    56|
    57|- [[demand-sent-by-prior-firm]] — when the prior firm already sent a demand
    58|- [[treatment-gap-defense]] — prior counsel cases often have treatment gaps that adjusters exploit
    59|", "total_lines": 58, "file_size": 3027, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "    50|- Half-day sessions are common; can be via Zoom
    51|- Mediation may be cancelled and rescheduled
    52|
    53|### 5. Expert Coordination
    54|- FCE and IME reports ordered during litigation
    55|- Expert invoices must be tracked and paid
    56|- Reports may need amendments as treatment progresses
    57|
    58|### Court Filing System
    59|- Kentucky courts use eFiling with automated NEF (Notice of Electronic Filing) and NCP notifications
    60|- Multiple attorneys and paralegals receive notifications simultaneously
    61|
    62|## Evidence
    63|
    64|- In a premises liability case in litigation (Knox Circuit Court), the litigation spanned 3+ years (2022-2025+). Activities included: complaint filing with IROG, checking service, receiving motions, joint deadline extension motions, mediation scheduling and cancellation, IME amendment requests, FCE scheduling and rescheduling, economist report ordering, deposition scheduling, subpoena responses, and supplemental discovery responses.
    65|- Motion hearings were rescheduled multiple times (from 1/20/22 to 2/14/22 via Facebook notice from clerk).
    66|- Certified records were required for litigation — regular copies insufficient. Certification forms had to be corrected (date range "04/2014 thru Current").
    67|- Mediation was scheduled through the National Academy of Distinguished Neutrals (NADN) with a retired judge mediator, coordinated with defense counsel schedules.
    68|- In Case G (MVA, Boone County), complaint filed ~1 year post-accident. Full litigation lifecycle including discovery exchanges with defense counsel (Richardson Law Group), deposition scheduling via Kentuckiana Reporters, and mediation via Zoom. Mediation failed — defense offered "last best & final" amounts that were inadequate. Zoom mediation required coordination with clients on technical setup (muting, call-in numbers). The case remained active post-mediation failure.
    69|- In Case H (MVA, Barren County), complaint filed in Barren Circuit Court (23-CI-00659). The defendant's insurer (Kentucky Farm Bureau) filed a parallel subrogation action in District Court. Depositions were scheduled via Kentuckiana Reporters. Mediation was being scheduled via the same service. Service of process required checking via alias — "Check for Service - Issue Alias is not perfected" — indicating the defendant was difficult to serve.
    70|- In Case I (MVA, Jefferson Circuit Court), complaint filed ~8 months post-accident (24-CI-005774) due to disputed liability (BI only accepting 25%). Discovery followed, with defense counsel reviewing dash cam video. Depositions were scheduled via Zoom for both the client and a witness (companion passenger). Client nearly missed deposition — required multiple urgent texts ("Cecil, you good?", "Cecil you ready?", "Cecil?") before responding. Deposition completed successfully. Defense counsel served interrogatories and additional discovery.
    71|- In Case F (WC case), litigation followed a different track — DWC (Department of Workers' Compensation) proceedings with BRC (Benefit Review Conferences) scheduled telephonically. Defense counsel (Pohl & Aubrey) exchanged correspondence about Form 106 requests and IME scheduling. This WC litigation model is distinct from civil circuit court litigation.
    72|- In Case F (auto accident, litigation), discovery involved interrogatories and requests for production exchanged between plaintiff and defendant. Defense counsel (Dilbeck & Myers) engaged actively in scheduling depositions. The defendant's deposition was scheduled via Zoom, with the plaintiff's deposition to follow immediately after. The firm communicated with the client via text and phone to prepare for deposition. Defense counsel's responses to production requests were tracked through email exchanges.
    73|- In Case G (auto accident, UIM litigation), a UIM complaint was filed separately against the client's own insurer (Allstate) in Fayette Circuit Court. The insurer filed an answer to the complaint. Discovery commenced with the plaintiff's first set of discovery. The UIM adjuster received a courtesy copy of the complaint. This demonstrates that UIM litigation follows a parallel track to BI claims.
    74|- In Case H (auto accident, litigation with stolen vehicle), the case (22-CI-002878) involved multiple defendants and defense counsel from different firms. Key litigation events included: defense counsel requesting and receiving a 60-day extension to answer the complaint while attempting to get the insurer to accept coverage; an Examination Under Oath (EUO) of the defendant regarding the stolen vehicle claim; deposition scheduling disputes where defense counsel was "adamant" about timing constraints; unilateral scheduling of depositions when counsel could not agree; and audio/video recording requests from the court. The case lasted 4+ years from DOI through ongoing litigation.
    75|- In a multi-defendant auto accident case in litigation (Jefferson Circuit Court, 21-CI-004985, DOI 1/2021), the litigation spanned 4+ years (2021-2025+). Activities included: complaint filing, interrogatory responses to defense counsel, deposition scheduling with defense counsel, State Farm motion to dismiss (AOC 280 Final Submission for Adjudication), certified medical records collection via ChartSwap, government open records requests for police body cam footage (via Axon/Evidence.com), multiple ORRs to LMPD, amended subpoenas (to Verizon and Metro PCS for phone records), Louisville Metro public records requests, forwarding evidence to defense counsel (Dinsmore, Smith & Hoskins), and locating witnesses (other vehicle passengers). This is the most complex litigation case observed, involving multiple defense counsel, government records, and electronic evidence. The 4+ year timeline from DOI to ongoing litigation confirms that multi-defendant cases with uninsured drivers dramatically extend case duration.
    76|
    77|## Agent Guidance
    78|
    79|1. Create a litigation calendar at filing with all deadlines from the court's scheduling order
    80|2. Track every court notification (NEF, NCP) and file appropriately
    81|3. Maintain a discovery checklist: what's been requested, what's been produced, what's outstanding
    82|4. For supplemental discovery responses, track what new information needs to be disclosed
    83|5. Coordinate expert scheduling well in advance — FCE and IME providers have limited availability
    84|6. When mediator sends a Zoom link or scheduling confirmation, immediately distribute to all parties
    85|7. Monitor motion hearing schedule — courts frequently reschedule
    86|8. Keep a running litigation expense tracker (expert fees, filing fees, mediator fees)
    87|
    88|## Pitfalls
    89|
    90|- Missing court deadlines (can result in sanctions or adverse rulings)
    91|- Not tracking supplemental discovery obligations
    92|- Allowing expert reports to become stale (e.g., new treatment occurred after IME)
    93|- Not verifying certified records meet litigation requirements
    94|- Failing to respond to mediator scheduling promptly (can delay case months)
    95|- Not updating the trial calendar when deadlines change
    96|
    97|## Related
    98|
    99|- [[premises-liability-case-patterns]] — PL cases are the most common path to litigation
   100|- [[statute-of-limitations-monitoring]] — SOL is the hard deadline for filing a complaint
   101|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — all litigation decisions require attorney involvement
   102|- [[medical-records-collection]] — litigation requires certified records, not just copies

# Dog Bite Case Patterns

> Dog bite cases follow a distinct pattern from auto accidents: liability depends on identifying the animal owner and their homeowner's insurance (not auto insurance), the client is frequently a minor (requiring guardian communication), and identifying insurance coverage can be a prolonged investigation. These cases may require creative investigation (address searches, certified mail to property/animal owners) and frequently proceed to litigation when the dog owner is uncooperative or uninsured.

## Pattern

### Liability Investigation
Dog bite cases require identifying:
1. **The animal owner** — may be different from the property owner where the attack occurred
2. **The property owner** — relevant for property liability theories
3. **Homeowner's insurance** — the primary coverage source (not auto insurance)

Investigation tools include:
- Address searches to identify residents at the animal's location
- Certified mail to property/dog owners requesting insurance information (LOR with turnover letter)
- Return receipt tracking (green cards) — if unclaimed, must resend to alternate addresses
- Direct contact with identified parties to request homeowner's insurance card
- Video evidence from home security cameras (e.g., Vivint) obtained from neighbors

### Insurance Identification Challenge
Unlike auto accidents where insurance is identified through the police report, dog bite cases require the firm to independently identify insurance:
- Send LOR/turnover letter to animal owner via certified mail
- Allow 30 days for defendant to provide insurance information
- If no response, investigate property owner (who may be different from animal owner)
- Homeowner's insurance may not be immediately identifiable — multiple attempts may be needed

### Minor Client Considerations
Dog bite clients are frequently minors, creating special requirements:
- Guardian (typically parent) is the primary point of contact
- All communication goes through the guardian
- Settlement of a minor's claim may require court approval
- Both guardian and minor may need to be deposed in litigation

### Treatment Pattern
- ER visit for initial treatment (wound care, infection check)
- Follow-up with PCP/pediatrician within days
- Monitoring for infection/inflammation
- Treatment is often brief compared to auto accident cases (1-2 provider visits)

## Evidence

- In a dog bite case involving a minor (DOI 8/2022), the firm identified the animal's location (adjacent property), performed an address search to identify residents, and sent certified mail LOR to the identified occupants. The first certified letter was unclaimed and returned; a second letter was signed by a defendant. After 30+ days with no insurance response, the firm texted the defendant directly requesting a photo of their homeowner's insurance card. Insurance was eventually identified as a regional farm bureau carrier. The guardian provided video evidence from a neighbor's home security system showing the animal running loose and attacking the client. The case moved to litigation when settlement appeared insufficient. Depositions of both guardian and minor were scheduled, cancelled by defense counsel, then rescheduled. A jury trial date was ultimately set. The case spanned 3+ years from DOI to ongoing litigation.

## Agent Guidance

1. At intake for dog bite cases, immediately confirm the animal's location address and identify all residents/property owners
2. Perform address search to identify potential defendants
3. Send LOR + turnover letter via certified mail to all identified parties
4. Track certified mail via USPS — if unclaimed, attempt alternative addresses
5. Allow 30 days for insurance response, then escalate (text, call, or investigate property owner separately)
6. Request video/photo evidence from the guardian immediately (neighbors may have security cameras)
7. Communicate exclusively through the guardian when the client is a minor
8. Prepare for litigation: dog bite cases with uncooperative owners frequently require complaint filing
9. When scheduling depositions for minor clients, coordinate with guardian for both depositions

## Pitfalls

- Assuming the animal owner has homeowner's insurance — they may be a renter without coverage
- Not distinguishing between the animal owner and the property owner
- Failing to preserve video evidence promptly — home security systems may overwrite footage
- Not tracking certified mail return receipts — proof of service may be needed for litigation
- Communicating with the minor client directly instead of through the guardian
- Underestimating case value because injuries appear minor — scarring and emotional impact may be significant

## Related

- [[premises-liability-case-patterns]] — similar property-based liability analysis
- [[client-communication-through-family]] — guardian communication is the norm in minor client cases
- [[litigation-process-patterns]] — dog bite cases frequently enter litigation
- [[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]] — modified intake process for non-auto cases
"}

{"path": "/opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/client-death-during-proceedings.md", "content": "---
title: Client Death During Active Proceedings
slug: client-death-during-proceedings
phase: [phase_7_litigation]

# Client Death During Active Proceedings

> In rare cases, a client may die while their case is in active litigation. This creates immediate procedural requirements: the firm must notify opposing counsel, cancel scheduled proceedings (depositions, IMEs), and determine whether the claim survives the client's death (which depends on case type and jurisdiction). The firm typically learns of the death through a family member contact.

## Pattern

### Discovery of Death
- A family member (parent, spouse, child) contacts the firm, often via voicemail to the assigned attorney
- The caller may reach the firm's general line or an attorney who doesn't primarily handle the case
- The family member may reference the client's next scheduled event (e.g., "He was supposed to be there for a deposition tomorrow")

### Immediate Actions Required
1. Verify the information with the family contact
2. Notify defense counsel immediately (same day, via email)
3. Cancel all scheduled proceedings (depositions, IMEs, hearings)
4. Notify the court if applicable
5. Consult with the attorney on claim survivability

### Claim Survivability
- **Workers' compensation**: WC death benefits may be available to dependents; the claim may convert from a personal injury WC claim to a death benefit claim
- **Personal injury (PI)**: In Kentucky, wrongful death and survival actions may apply if the death is related to the injury; the estate must be substituted as plaintiff
- **Unrelated death**: If the death is unrelated to the case injury, the personal injury claim may still survive through the estate under survival statute provisions

### Estate Substitution
- An estate representative (executor/administrator) must be identified
- Motion to substitute the estate as plaintiff may be required
- Probate proceedings may need to be initiated if no estate exists

## Evidence

- In a WC case (workplace injury at automotive plant, DOI 5/2023), the client passed away on 11/2/2025. The client's mother left a voicemail the day before the scheduled deposition (11/14/2025): "My son was supposed to be there for a deposition tomorrow. He has passed away..." The assigned attorney sent an email to defense counsel that same evening notifying them of the death and requesting a call to discuss next steps. The most recent substantive filing before the death was a document filed via LMS. The case remained open as of the last activity log, indicating procedural resolution was still pending.

## Agent Guidance

1. If a family member calls reporting a client's death, treat as highest priority — route to the assigned attorney immediately
2. Document the caller's name, relationship, contact information, and date of death
3. Do NOT discuss case details with the family member until attorney authorization
4. Cancel all scheduled proceedings within 24 hours
5. Send written notification to all opposing counsel
6. Flag the case for attorney review regarding claim survivability
7. If an estate needs to be opened, coordinate with the family and the attorney on probate requirements

## Pitfalls

- Delaying notification to opposing counsel — this can create ethical issues
- Discussing settlement or case value with the grieving family member
- Assuming the claim dies with the client — many claims survive through the estate
- Failing to cancel scheduled proceedings (depositions, IMEs, hearings)
- Not documenting the notification chain (who was told, when, how)

## Related

- [[workers-compensation-case-patterns]] — WC cases have specific death benefit provisions
- [[litigation-process-patterns]] — death during litigation requires procedural pivots
- [[case-phase-transitions]] — client death represents a unique terminal or transitional event
"}

{"content": "    30|### Multi-Channel Escalation
    31|The firm follows a predictable escalation pattern when a client is unreachable:
    32|1. **Day 1**: Outbound call → no answer / voicemail full
    33|2. **Day 1**: Text message sent as fallback
    34|3. **Day 2-3**: Second outbound call attempt + email
    35|4. **Week 2**: Follow-up task created ("Two Week follow up with client")
    36|5. **Week 3-4**: Additional call attempts, emails, texts
    37|6. **Ongoing**: "Still unreachable" logged in case notes
    38|7. **Eventually**: Client responds via a different channel or new phone number
    39|
    40|### Impact on Case Timeline
    41|- Intake tasks cannot be completed without client input (providers list, insurance card, SSN)
    42|- File Setup stalls — the case sits in File Setup for months instead of progressing
    43|- Medical records requests are delayed because providers aren't confirmed
    44|- The case may ultimately be declined partly due to the difficulty of working it without client cooperation
    45|
    46|### Client Eventually Resurfaces
    47|Unreachable clients often do eventually make contact, sometimes with a new phone number. When they do:
    48|- They expect the case to be progressing despite their absence
    49|- They may provide the requested information belatedly
    50|- The firm must quickly re-engage and catch up on delayed tasks
    51|
    52|## Evidence
    53|
    54|- In an auto accident case (DOI 3/2024), the client was unreachable from day one of file setup. Multiple calls were made with the note "No answer, vm is full." Text messages were sent as fallback. The client eventually responded via text ("I will call you later, thanks") and then called back from a different number. The voicemail transcription showed they had also been trying to reach the firm: "We have missed each other for the last day or 2." Despite repeated outreach attempts over weeks, the client remained difficult to reach throughout the case. By 2/2025, the note still read "Still unreachable." The client's phone number changed at least once during the case.
    55|- In the same case, even when the firm was trying to close/decline the case, a staff member noted they needed to speak with the client about the case update — and had to use a different phone number than the one on file.
    56|
    57|## Agent Guidance
    58|
    59|1. If the first call goes to voicemail and VM is full, immediately send a text message

{"content": "    50|- Settlement offer communicated without full explanation
    51|- Being told the case is "on settlement" without a timeline
    52|- Calls going unreturned or to voicemail
    53|
    54|## Evidence
    55|
    56|- In an auto accident case (negotiation phase), the client's frustration escalated over 12+ months:
    57|  - 6/2024: "Hey are you going to get back with me or do I need to take another route. Let me know today"
    58|  - 3/2025: Voicemail — "will you please get back in contact with me... you keep in touch with me or nothing... call me back today and just give me something. I told you I am not settling"
    59|  - 3/2025-3/2026: Multiple missed calls logged (3/21/2025 alone had 2 missed calls + 1 outbound call + texts)
    60|  - 8/2025: Multiple missed calls to different staff members
    61|  - 2/2026, 3/2026: Continued missed calls with empty voicemail transcriptions
    62|- In a premises liability case, the client texted the attorney directly: "Haven't heard anything from you or an update since court on Monday, and just wanted to reach out" — followed by the attorney providing a substantive update about next steps.
    63|- In Case H (MVA, air evac, 2.5+ year case), the client communicated through a go-between (Ann Dattillo). Client called for updates repeatedly — "said she had not heard from anyone recently." Client was confused about deposition process ("said that her attorney was to come out to her residence"). After attorney departure, client needed reassurance about new point of contact. The intermediary requested a copy of the deposition and case update. This shows Stage 2-3 frustration compounded by staff turnover.
    64|- In Case I (MVA, disputed liability, litigation), the client's calls went unreturned for extended periods — 4 missed calls logged between Feb-Mar 2026 with no callback documented. Client had previously been responsive and cooperative (provided provider info via text, completed deposition). The communication breakdown occurred post-deposition when the case was in a waiting period. This confirms the pattern that frustration peaks during litigation gaps.
    65|- In Case H, client communication was relayed through "Anna per Client instructions" — the intermediary called for updates when the client felt out of the loop. After Bryce Koon's departure from the firm, the case was reassigned, and the client needed to be re-introduced to the new attorney — a staff transition that exacerbated the frustration pattern.
    66|- In Case F (auto accident, litigation), the client called multiple staff members repeatedly during active litigation (6/2025-3/2026): missed calls to the associate attorney, then to the lead attorney, then to the paralegal — all on the same day (3/3/2026). The voicemail was forwarded internally, and the attorney returned the call the same day. This demonstrates that even when substantive litigation activity is ongoing (depositions being scheduled), clients still escalate if they are not proactively informed.
    67|- In Case G (auto accident, UIM litigation), the client showed Stage 1 behavior at 6/2025 (calling about documents she expected but hadn't received), followed by steady escalation with missed calls to multiple staff members (7/2025, 8/2025 — 3 missed calls in 8 minutes on 8/22/2025), continuing through 12/2025-1/2026. The case had $31K in pre-settlement funding creating additional financial pressure.
    68|- In Case H (auto accident, 4+ year litigation), the client texted "Good morning was reaching out for an update" (3/2025) and "Hello was wondering if we had an update on our case haven't heard anything since March" (5/2025). The firm responded within a day with specific information about deposition scheduling. This shows that proactive updates after court events prevent escalation.
    69|- In a workers' compensation case (workplace injury, treatment phase), the client sent emails with increasingly urgent subject lines: "ANY UPDATES?" (8/2025) and "UPDATES??" (10/2025). The escalating punctuation and capitalization follow the same trajectory as the phone-based pattern. The firm responded to each, but the repeated pattern over months confirms that even cases in active treatment (not just negotiation) can trigger client frustration when the client perceives a lack of forward progress. The client also asked about deposition scheduling details and case status, suggesting the frustration was compounded by not understanding the WC litigation process.
    70|- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff, negotiation/litigation), the client's frustration followed a textbook escalation: (1) polite inquiry phase with regular updates from the firm (Aug-Dec 2023), (2) frustration peaked when the client was told "we are still waiting on [another plaintiff's] medical bills" repeatedly over months (Jan-Apr 2024), (3) the client called to complain about "not receiving call backs or being told conflicting information repeatedly" — she came to the office in person to demand an update (Feb 2024), (4) the attorney spoke with her directly and calmed the situation. The pattern continued in the litigation phase with multiple missed calls (Sep-Nov 2025), text message difficulties ("Can u you give me a call it won't lemme call you"), and the attorney texting back and forth to coordinate. This case uniquely shows frustration driven by inter-counsel coordination delays — the client's case was ready but held hostage by another firm's inaction.
    71|- In Case N (premises liability, estate case, 5+ year litigation), the surviving spouse called for updates repeatedly throughout 2025-2026. The pattern showed: July 2025, the spouse called saying he "wants to speak with attorney" and "has not rec'd a return call." The firm provided an update via the paralegal. Missed calls continued through Sep 2025, Oct 2025 (two missed calls same day), Dec 2025 (two more missed calls), and Mar 2026. This demonstrates that estate cases, where the primary contact is a surviving family member rather than the injured party, follow the same frustration escalation. The emotional dimension is heightened — the spouse is dealing with both grief and the legal process. The 5+ year timeline makes sustained communication even more critical.
    72|
    73|## Agent Guidance
    74|
    75|1. Set automated 2-week check-in reminders for every active case in demand/negotiation phase
    76|2. When a client calls and you cannot provide an update, say WHEN they can expect one (e.g., "I will have an update by Friday")
    77|3. If you cannot reach the client (their voicemail is full or not set up), send a text immediately
    78|4. When a client shows Stage 2 behavior, escalate to the attorney for a personal call within 24 hours
    79|5. Never tell a client the case is "on settlement" unless the client has already agreed to the amount

3. `litigation-process-patterns` — evidence_count: 5->9, confidence: medium (added 4 new litigation cases: PL emergency filing, dog bite with trial date, elderly MVA demand-in-litigation, WC client death)

5. `unreachable-client-patterns` — evidence_count: 2->4 (added reverse unreachability pattern, bidirectional voicemail-full)
6. `medical-records-collection` — evidence_count: 10->13, confidence: medium->high (added 15+ provider PL case, elderly patient 21+ providers, WC multi-provider treatment)
7. `case-phase-transitions` — evidence_count: 11->15, confidence: medium->high (added same-day PNC->File Setup->Litigation, BI-settled->complaint-filed, WC monitoring->litigation, WC death event)
8. `intake-and-onboarding-workflow` — evidence_count: 10->14, confidence: medium->high (added transfer case with SASE, dog bite with guardian intake, WC VineSign intake delay, elderly MVA with prior offer)
9. `workers-compensation-case-patterns` — evidence_count: 1->2 (added Ford WC case with monitoring posture, client death, deposition/IME scheduling)
10. `workers-compensation-claim-patterns` — evidence_count: 1->2 (added TTD monitoring, defense-initiated litigation)
11. `client-communication-through-family` — evidence_count: 5->7, confidence: medium->high (added dog bite guardian/minor, WC mother notification of death)
12. `statute-of-limitations-monitoring` — evidence_count: 5->7, confidence: medium->high (added <60 day SOL emergency, dog bite 1-year SOL with litigation)
13. `expert-reports-and-evaluations` — evidence_count: 3->5, confidence: low->medium (added EvenUP for case valuation, WC IME + Commonwealth IME expenses)
14. `pip-claims-management` — evidence_count: 5->6 (added KFB PIP in elderly MVA with Medicare)
15. `lien-management-health-insurance` — evidence_count: 5->6 (added multiple health insurer liens in PL case: Anthem, BCBS Michigan, Humana)

### Connections Updated
1. `client-frustration-drives-premature-settlement` — evidence_count: 4->7
2. `premises-liability-requires-litigation-prep` — evidence_count: 1->2
3. `litigation-as-negotiation-leverage` — evidence_count: 4->6

### Confidence Upgrades (7 articles)
- `client-frustration-escalation`: medium -> high (10 cases)
- `medical-records-collection`: medium -> high (13 cases)
- `case-phase-transitions`: medium -> high (15 cases)
- `intake-and-onboarding-workflow`: medium -> high (14 cases)
- `client-communication-through-family`: medium -> high (7 cases)
- `statute-of-limitations-monitoring`: medium -> high (7 cases)
- `expert-reports-and-evaluations`: low -> medium (5 cases)

### Key New Patterns Identified
- **Dog bite cases follow a distinct liability investigation pattern**: Unlike auto accidents, the firm must identify the animal owner, property owner, and homeowner's insurance through address searches and certified mail — a process that can take months
- **SOL pressure on transfer cases creates emergency filing**: When a case is transferred from prior counsel with SOL approaching (<60 days), the firm may need to onboard and file a complaint on the same day — skipping demand entirely
- **Client death during proceedings is a real edge case**: A WC client died before their deposition, requiring immediate procedural pivots and family notification handling
- **Bidirectional unreachability exists**: In some cases, both the client AND the firm are unreachable to each other (client calls go unanswered, firm's return calls go to voicemail full) — creating complete communication breakdown
- **Guardian communication in minor's cases follows parent-proxy patterns**: In dog bite cases with minor clients, the guardian handles all communication, court appearances, and deposition scheduling
- **WC monitoring posture is a valid strategy**: Some WC cases are monitored passively (no LOR sent, no active participation) while the client receives TTD and treatment through the WC carrier, with the firm only engaging when litigation begins
- **EvenUP is used as a case evaluation tool**: In at least one case, EvenUP expenses indicate it was used for AI-assisted case valuation during litigation

### Remaining Gaps
- Settlement disbursement: Still no cases reached completed disbursement
- Dog bite case valuations: Need settlement data for dog bite cases
- Client death claim survivability outcomes: Need to see how the WC death case resolves
- Estate substitution procedures: Only observed in WC context, not PI
- Medicare lien resolution: Medicare liens identified but no reduction/payment data
- EvenUP impact on outcomes: Tool cost documented but impact on settlement unknown"}
